title: Data Update 7 for 2026: Debt and Taxes
gist: Damodaran lays out the real tax-benefit-vs-bankruptcy-cost tradeoff behind corporate debt, then applies it to 2025 data showing calm-on-the-surface default rates masking a May 2025 default-probability spike, and argues that AI capex is increasingly funded by debt (including $200B+ of private credit), meaning the coming 'Big Market Delusion' correction in AI will hit credit markets, not just equity holders.

insights.claim: The claim that 'debt is cheaper than equity' is an illusion once you account for the fact that fairly-priced debt raises the riskiness of equity, leaving the overall cost of capital unchanged (no default risk) or higher (with default risk) rather than creating a free lunch.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Investors should discount management rationales for leverage that cite 'cheap debt' alone; the real payoff only shows up through tax benefits, not through financing mechanics.
insights.quote: you should do so, but dispense with the illusion that this is a free lunch
insights.locator: Debt versus Equity: Choices and Tradeoff

insights.claim: Borrowing to boost return on equity is real, but it symmetrically raises the cost of equity and magnifies both successful and failed outcomes for shareholders.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: High-ROE companies achieved via leverage should be judged net of the added equity risk, not treated as pure value creation.
insights.locator: Among risk-takers, a common reason for using debt

insights.claim: A BBB-rated company that borrows and captures tax advantages can be worth more than an identical AAA-rated company carrying no debt, since bond-rating optimization is not the same objective as value maximization.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Investors and boards fixating on credit-rating preservation may be destroying value by under-levering.
insights.quote: a BBB-rated firm that borrows money and gets tax advantages can be worth more than the same firm with a AAA rating and no debt
insights.locator: Those who avoid debt have their own share of illusions

insights.claim: The core determinant of how much a company should borrow is the tradeoff between the tax benefits of debt and the expected costs of bankruptcy risk, which Damodaran calls a simple but powerful explainer of corporate borrowing levels.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Use this two-variable lens (marginal tax rate, default risk) as a quick screen for whether a company's actual leverage looks rational or distorted.
insights.quote: The tax benefits versus bankruptcy cost trade off on debt is a simple and very powerful explainer of how much companies should borrow
insights.locator: So what are the real trade offs?

themes.theme: Why companies deviate from the optimal debt level

insights.claim: Implicit or explicit government guarantees (e.g., Korean companies in the 1990s, or firms perceived as too-big-to-fail) remove the fear of default and push companies to over-borrow relative to fundamentals.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Sectors or national champions with implicit state backstops should be expected to run structurally higher leverage than fundamentals justify — a useful cross-check when comparing credit spreads across regions.
insights.locator: Shields against bankruptcy

insights.claim: Founders and family-controlled businesses often over-borrow relative to what fundamentals support because raising equity means ceding ownership/control, which explains debt use even in regions like the Middle East where interest is not tax-deductible.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Screening for founder/family control alongside above-average leverage-with-no-tax-benefit can flag control-motivated (not value-motivated) capital structures.
insights.locator: Control versus Value

insights.claim: Venture debt — lending to start-ups and money-losing companies with unformed business models — is, in Damodaran's view, an almost absurd concept from a pure corporate-finance standpoint, existing mainly because it lets founders raise capital without diluting ownership.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Treat venture-debt-heavy young companies as carrying mispriced credit risk relative to their cash-flow reality.
insights.quote: the existence of venture debt , an almost absurd notion from a corporate finance standpoint, since you are lending to start-ups and young money-losing companies with unformed business models
insights.locator: Control versus Value

insights.claim: Below-market subsidized debt granted for perceived social purpose (e.g., green energy) or political/security priority (defense, infrastructure) leads those sectors to carry structurally more debt than fundamentals would otherwise support.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Elevated leverage in subsidized sectors is a policy artifact, not a signal of superior debt capacity — reassess if subsidies are withdrawn.
insights.locator: Subsidized debt

insights.claim: Restrictive bank covenants are onerous enough that many technology companies choose to forgo bank debt entirely (or go straight to bond markets) even when they have large, stable cash flows and clear tax benefits available.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Explains why tech balance sheets look under-levered relative to textbook optimal capital structure — it's an operating-flexibility choice, not a tax-inefficiency.
insights.locator: Restrictive covenants

insights.claim: Companies with equity trading well above fundamental value will use their overpriced stock as acquisition currency instead of debt, even when they have unused debt capacity.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Watch for stock-funded M&A sprees among richly-valued companies as a tell of management (rationally) exploiting equity mispricing rather than optimizing capital structure.
insights.locator: Overpriced equity

insights.claim: Marginal corporate tax rates across the world's largest economies have converged around 25%, though outliers remain: Ireland at 12% in Europe and Paraguay at 10% in Latin America; Latin America and Africa have the highest regional rates, Eastern Europe and Russia the lowest.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Tax-rate arbitrage opportunities are narrowing among major economies but remain material at specific low-tax jurisdictions used for domicile/profit-shifting decisions.
insights.locator: Marginal Tax Rates

insights.claim: Effective tax rates were lower than marginal tax rates for roughly 60% of companies in Damodaran's global sample, which he treats as a rough proxy for how effective (loophole-laden) a country's tax system is.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A wide marginal-vs-effective gap by country is a usable screen for where multinational tax-planning upside is largest.
insights.locator: Effective tax rates

insights.claim: India has the least 'effective' tax code among regions studied, with an average effective tax rate of 22.33% against a 30% marginal rate, followed by the US and Japan, partly reflecting lower-taxed foreign sales.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Companies domiciled in India, the US, or Japan with large foreign-sales mix have more room to compress effective tax rates below statutory levels.
insights.locator: In the aggregate, the effective tax rates were lower

insights.claim: In 2025 the average cash tax rate was 25.86% for global companies and 21.02% for US companies, both roughly 1 percentage point above the corresponding effective (accrual) tax rates.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Cash taxes running slightly ahead of book taxes suggests limited near-term cash-tax relief from deferral strategies for the average firm.
insights.locator: Cash tax rates

themes.theme: Default risk: calm surface, disquiet underneath

insights.claim: S&P recorded a decline in global corporate defaults from 145 in 2024 to 117 in 2025, with the US share of global defaults falling from 67% to 62%, i.e., the US drove most of the improvement.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Headline default data for 2025 reads as benign, but the composition (US-led improvement) matters for regional credit allocation.
insights.locator: S&P, in its review of 2025 default and distress

insights.claim: Despite falling default counts, Moody's estimate of the probability of default for US companies spiked to 9.2% in May 2025 — its highest level since the 2008 financial crisis — signaling stress beneath a reassuring headline default rate.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A single-month PD spike to post-2008 highs is a leading indicator worth tracking even when trailing default counts look fine; it hints credit conditions were more fragile in 2025 than realized defaults suggest.
insights.locator: In May 2025, Moody's estimate of the probability of default

insights.claim: 2025 saw more bond-rating downgrades than upgrades, with almost $60 billion of corporate bonds falling below investment grade during the year.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Rising fallen-angel volume feeds high-yield supply and can pressure credit spreads even amid low headline default counts.
insights.locator: On the bond ratings front

insights.claim: Only two AAA-rated companies remained in the world at the start of 2026 according to S&P: Johnson & Johnson and Microsoft; the US has the highest share of listed companies carrying any bond rating (11.43%) yet also the highest share of below-investment-grade ratings among rated firms, implying self-selection elsewhere in the world where only firms expecting strong ratings bother to seek one.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Cross-country comparisons of average bond ratings are distorted by this selection bias — don't read a region's higher average rating as lower true credit risk.
insights.locator: Finally, at the start of 2026

themes.theme: Debt burden by sector: tech's fortress balance sheets

insights.claim: Technology companies carry the largest debt-safety buffers of any sector globally and in the US, with the highest interest coverage ratios and lowest debt-to-EBITDA, while real estate and utilities carry the least buffer (lowest coverage, highest debt-to-EBITDA).
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Sector-level credit risk screens should treat tech as structurally low-default-risk on a debt-comfort basis, and real estate/utilities as structurally more exposed to rate and refinancing risk.
insights.locator: As you can see, with both the US and global groupings

insights.claim: On a market-value basis, technology companies carry only 3-5% debt relative to capital, and book-value debt ratios substantially overstate true leverage for this sector because accountants fail to capitalize tech's biggest (often intangible) assets.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Analysts relying on book-value leverage ratios will systematically overstate tech-sector financial risk versus the market-value reality.
insights.locator: As you can see, companies look significantly more debt-laden

insights.claim: Book-value debt ratios should never be used in financial/valuation analysis because they are dated and distorted by accounting conventions, whereas market-value ratios correctly reflect what it would cost to acquire the firm today.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Discount any leverage analysis or covenant framework built purely on book-value debt-to-capital.
insights.locator: At the risk of sounding dogmatic

insights.claim: Collective AI-related capital expenditure and cash acquisitions at Meta, Alphabet, Amazon, Oracle, Microsoft and Broadcom hit $400 billion in 2025, marking a dramatic shift from capital-light to capital-intensive business models over the 2015-2025 period, with company guidance pointing to further increases.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The capex ramp is not plateauing per guidance, so free-cash-flow available for buybacks/dividends at these names will keep compressing.
insights.locator: The shift at these firms from capital-light to capital-intensive models

insights.claim: Even the cash-rich big tech companies turned to debt in 2025, collectively borrowing $160 billion to help fund AI investment, though Damodaran says this scale of debt does not put them at meaningful risk given their cash flows and market caps.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Big tech's own borrowing is a solvency non-issue, but it signals capex needs now exceed what internal cash generation alone can fund, a trend to watch as it compounds.
insights.quote: In 2025, the big tech companies collectively borrowed $160 billion, but given their cashflows and market capitalization, that debt does not put them at risk
insights.locator: In 2025, the big tech companies collectively borrowed $160 billion

insights.claim: Damodaran argues AI investment collectively reflects a 'Big Market Delusion' — overconfidence and fear of being left behind driving both corporate capex and investor pricing — the same pattern seen in dot-com stocks in the 1990s, online advertising companies roughly a decade ago, and early cannabis stocks; the belief that only two or three big winners will emerge is fueling frenzied investment across far more entrants than the market can support, making a correction and clean-up inevitable.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Position for an eventual shakeout among AI infrastructure/model companies; the 'winner-take-most' narrative implies most current AI capex bets will not earn positive NPV.
insights.quote: This big market delusion is a feature, not a bug
insights.locator: AI Investing and Debt

themes.theme: Private credit, AI, and systemic risk

insights.claim: More than $200 billion in private debt went to AI firms in 2025, and AI-related lending is rising as a share of private credit portfolios overall, as private credit usage has exploded over the last two decades after being a niche option historically.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Private credit fund exposure to AI infrastructure is now large enough that an AI capex slowdown would be a credit event, not just an equity drawdown.
insights.locator: As AI has increasingly taken a starring role in markets

insights.claim: Private credit's stated advantages over banks/bond markets — better default-risk data, cash-flow-based (vs. asset-based) lending, and speed/customization — are legitimate mainly for smaller, private, less-established borrowers and specific projects, and Damodaran finds them unconvincing as justification for large tech companies, which already have fast, fairly-priced access to bank and bond markets (as shown by Google's hundred-year bond issuance).
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Be skeptical of private-credit pitches to large, liquid tech borrowers — the economic rationale for using private credit there is weak versus banks/bonds.
insights.locator: Clearly, these reasons for the presence of private debt have merit

insights.claim: Damodaran views the Blue Owl private-credit loan to Meta for AI investment as either a below-market-rate loan or one carrying terms no prudent lender would accept — and says neither is a sustainable long-term lending strategy.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Treat large private-credit deals with mega-cap AI spenders as a potential mispricing/covenant-quality red flag, not proof private credit has found a genuine edge.
insights.locator: When a private credit fund lends Meta for an AI investment

insights.claim: Because AI investment is now substantially debt-funded (especially via private credit), the eventual correction from the Big Market Delusion will not be contained to equity holders as in an all-equity bubble — it will reprice private credit down for higher default risk and spread pain through the broader economy, amounting to 'a banking crisis created primarily by non-banking lenders behaving badly.'
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The AI unwind's systemic risk channel runs through private credit repricing, not just equity drawdowns — watch private credit mark-downs and stress reports (S&P already flagged some in the past year) as the leading indicator.
insights.quote: In effect, we will have a banking crisis created primarily by non-banking lenders behaving badly
insights.locator: The coming together of the two storylines on AI and private credit

insights.claim: Private credit has delivered good returns but has been oversold as an alternative asset class to the point of becoming too big; Damodaran expects an overdue shakeout that separates sloppy lenders from good ones and shrinks the sector to healthier levels.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Expect consolidation/losses concentrated among the most aggressively-grown private credit funds; due diligence quality will separate survivors from casualties.
insights.quote: A shakeout is overdue, which will separate the sloppy lenders from the good ones, and perhaps shrink private credit to healthier levels
insights.locator: Private credit has had a good run
